text: Identifies that the father's promise is conditioned on the son's marriage to a specific person within 18 months, constituting an offer accepted by performance.
States the Statute of Frauds requires contracts made upon consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) to be in signed writing to be enforceable.
Correctly applies that the father's promise falls within the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds category because it is a third-party promise conditioned on marriage.
Discusses promissory estoppel as a possible but weak exception to the Statute of Frauds, noting weak inducement and courts' caution in overriding writing requirements.
Acknowledges the father's unilateral mistake about the availability of a tax deduction does not excuse performance absent express condition.
Concludes that the father's oral promise is not enforceable due to the Statute of Frauds marriage-consideration writing requirement and absence of writing.
Considers and addresses counterarguments such as promissory estoppel and main purpose exception, explaining their limited applicability.
Includes factual application that the son married within 14 months, satisfying the condition for acceptance by performance.
Avoids the error of treating the father's mistaken belief about tax deduction as a valid excuse for nonperformance.
The response addresses the principal counterargument or defense when relevant.
